APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses open-domain QA over Wikipedia — a central topic in NLP/ML — and proposes a complete retrieval + neural reader pipeline (TF-IDF with bigram hashing + 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader) evaluated on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) with EM/F1 metrics. The methods (retrieval augmentation, distant supervision, multitask learning) and comparisons to established baselines (ElasticSearch API, YodaQA, BiDAF, DCN) match community expectations and audience interests. Given its use of standard datasets, rigorous evaluation, and clear positioning within existing QA work, it is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue. | B(5): This is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: open-domain question answering over Wikipedia is a core NLP task and the paper combines retrieval and neural reading comprehension models (TF-IDF + bigram hashing retriever, 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader) evaluated on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies). The use of distant supervision and multitask learning to build an end-to-end system, reporting of hyperparameters, and thorough related work make the work relevant to the community and appropriate for the conference audience.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses open-domain QA over Wikipedia — a central topic in NLP/ML — and proposes a complete retrieval + neural reader pipeline (TF-IDF with bigram hashing + 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader) evaluated on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) with EM/F1 metrics. The methods (retrieval augmentation, distant supervision, multitask learning) and comparisons to established baselines (ElasticSearch API, YodaQA, BiDAF, DCN) match community expectations and audience interests. Given its use of standard datasets, rigorous evaluation, and clear positioning within existing QA work, it is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: open-domain question answering over Wikipedia is a core NLP task and the paper combines retrieval and neural reading comprehension models (TF-IDF + bigram hashing retriever, 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader) evaluated on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies). The use of distant supervision and multitask learning to build an end-to-end system, reporting of hyperparameters, and thorough related work make the work relevant to the community and appropriate for the conference audience.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides a broad experimental evaluation: retrieval comparisons (TF-IDF with bigram hashing vs Wikipedia Search API), a strong Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art on SQuAD, and experiments using distant supervision and multitask learning to build an end-to-end open-domain QA system over Wikipedia. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, hyperparameters are given, and several limitations (e.g., SQuAD’s paragraph-conditioned questions, lack of paragraph annotations in other datasets, and Wikipedia’s suitability for machines) are explicitly discussed. The main gap in the summary is an absence of explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses for model components and retrieval choices, which prevents finer-grained attribution of gains. | B(4): The paper runs a broad set of experiments: it empirically compares the TF–IDF+bigram retriever to the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, demonstrates a strong 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art SQuAD performance, and evaluates end-to-end QA using distant supervision and multitask learning on CuratedTREC, WebQuestions and WikiMovies. Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (SQuAD distributional issues, datasets lacking paragraphs, and Wikipedia’s human-oriented format). However, the summary does not explicitly report thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses for core design choices nor confirm code release, leaving some reproducibility and component-wise attribution questions unresolved.

Related work coverage is reported as thorough, hyperparameters are given, and several limitations (e.g., SQuAD’s paragraph-conditioned questions, lack of paragraph annotations in other datasets, and Wikipedia’s suitability for machines) are explicitly discussed. The main gap in the summary is an absence of explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses for model components and retrieval choices, which prevents finer-grained attribution of gains.

Related work is reported as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (SQuAD distributional issues, datasets lacking paragraphs, and Wikipedia’s human-oriented format). However, the summary does not explicitly report thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses for core design choices nor confirm code release, leaving some reproducibility and component-wise attribution questions unresolved.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper addresses a well-studied open-domain QA problem and mainly combines existing components (TF-IDF retrieval, bigram hashing, multilayer BiLSTM readers, distant supervision, and multitask learning). Its empirical findings—e.g., that a simple TF-IDF retriever with bigram hashing outperforms the Elasticsearch-based API and that the Document Reader achieves strong SQuAD results—are useful but constitute incremental advances rather than fundamentally new methods. Overall, the work clearly differentiates contributions and offers practical innovations, but the techniques are non-trivial extensions of prior work rather than wholly new ideas. | B(4): The paper does not invent entirely new model primitives but presents notable, non-trivial innovations: a simple TF–IDF retriever enhanced with bigram hashing that empirically outperforms the built-in ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, a multi-layer Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art on SQuAD, and a practical distant-supervision + multitask strategy to train an end-to-end open-domain QA system over Wikipedia. These contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work (e.g., BiDAF, coattention models) and amount to a meaningful advance in assembling and adapting existing techniques for the open-domain Wikipedia setting. While the approach largely combines known components, the empirical findings and engineering decisions provide useful and somewhat surprising insights to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied open-domain QA problem and mainly combines existing components (TF-IDF retrieval, bigram hashing, multilayer BiLSTM readers, distant supervision, and multitask learning). Its empirical findings—e.g., that a simple TF-IDF retriever with bigram hashing outperforms the Elasticsearch-based API and that the Document Reader achieves strong SQuAD results—are useful but constitute incremental advances rather than fundamentally new methods. Overall, the work clearly differentiates contributions and offers practical innovations, but the techniques are non-trivial extensions of prior work rather than wholly new ideas.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not invent entirely new model primitives but presents notable, non-trivial innovations: a simple TF–IDF retriever enhanced with bigram hashing that empirically outperforms the built-in ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, a multi-layer Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art on SQuAD, and a practical distant-supervision + multitask strategy to train an end-to-end open-domain QA system over Wikipedia. These contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work (e.g., BiDAF, coattention models) and amount to a meaningful advance in assembling and adapting existing techniques for the open-domain Wikipedia setting. While the approach largely combines known components, the empirical findings and engineering decisions provide useful and somewhat surprising insights to experts.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a central problem—open-domain question answering over a large, real-world corpus—and demonstrates practical, high-impact techniques: a simple TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing that outperforms the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API and a 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art SQuAD performance. By using distant supervision and multitask training to build an end-to-end system over the full Wikipedia dump, it convincingly shows that scalable retrieval plus neural machine comprehension is a viable approach for open-domain QA. These results are likely to change how practitioners build QA pipelines and to spawn follow-up work on retrieval-reader integration, but they are more evolutionary (strong and widely useful) than wholly transformative to the field. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of open-domain QA over a large, real-world knowledge source (Wikipedia) and demonstrates practical, high-impact techniques: a simple TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing that outperforms the built-in Wikipedia Search API and a Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art SQuAD performance. Its use of distant supervision and multitask learning to train an end-to-end Wikipedia QA system makes a convincing case that retrieval+machine-reading pipelines can scale to open-domain settings. These results are likely to influence how researchers build and train QA systems and to be widely adopted, though dataset-specific limitations (e.g., SQuAD’s paragraph-conditioned questions) temper a fully transformative claim.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a central problem—open-domain question answering over a large, real-world corpus—and demonstrates practical, high-impact techniques: a simple TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing that outperforms the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API and a 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art SQuAD performance. By using distant supervision and multitask training to build an end-to-end system over the full Wikipedia dump, it convincingly shows that scalable retrieval plus neural machine comprehension is a viable approach for open-domain QA. These results are likely to change how practitioners build QA pipelines and to spawn follow-up work on retrieval-reader integration, but they are more evolutionary (strong and widely useful) than wholly transformative to the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of open-domain QA over a large, real-world knowledge source (Wikipedia) and demonstrates practical, high-impact techniques: a simple TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing that outperforms the built-in Wikipedia Search API and a Document Reader that achieves state-of-the-art SQuAD performance. Its use of distant supervision and multitask learning to train an end-to-end Wikipedia QA system makes a convincing case that retrieval+machine-reading pipelines can scale to open-domain settings. These results are likely to influence how researchers build and train QA systems and to be widely adopted, though dataset-specific limitations (e.g., SQuAD’s paragraph-conditioned questions) temper a fully transformative claim.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (ElasticSearch Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, Dynamic Coattention, BiDAF, Multi-Perspective Matching) and evaluates on standard datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) using EM/F1. Comparisons seem largely fair — same datasets/metrics, hyperparameters reported — and the paper analyzes key differences (TF‑IDF+bigram retriever vs. ElasticSearch, the Document Reader architecture, and distant‑supervision/multi-task training). However, the summary does not make explicit that all baselines were evaluated under identical open‑domain conditions (e.g., distant supervision for paragraphless datasets), nor does it explicitly state when prior methods perform comparably on particular settings. Related work coverage is described as thorough. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines including the ElasticSearch Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, and strong reading models (Dynamic Coattn, BiDAF, Multi-Perspective Matching), and evaluates on standard datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) using common metrics (EM/F1). Comparisons appear fair: same datasets/metrics, hyperparameters reported, and the authors explicitly acknowledge dataset limitations (SQuAD’s paragraph-conditioned questions and lack of paragraphs in other datasets) and use distant supervision/multitask learning to address them. Related work coverage is described as thorough and prior methods’ performance is acknowledged where relevant.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (ElasticSearch Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, Dynamic Coattention, BiDAF, Multi-Perspective Matching) and evaluates on standard datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) using EM/F1. Comparisons seem largely fair — same datasets/metrics, hyperparameters reported — and the paper analyzes key differences (TF‑IDF+bigram retriever vs. ElasticSearch, the Document Reader architecture, and distant‑supervision/multi-task training). However, the summary does not make explicit that all baselines were evaluated under identical open‑domain conditions (e.g., distant supervision for paragraphless datasets), nor does it explicitly state when prior methods perform comparably on particular settings. Related work coverage is described as thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines including the ElasticSearch Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, and strong reading models (Dynamic Coattn, BiDAF, Multi-Perspective Matching), and evaluates on standard datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) using common metrics (EM/F1). Comparisons appear fair: same datasets/metrics, hyperparameters reported, and the authors explicitly acknowledge dataset limitations (SQuAD’s paragraph-conditioned questions and lack of paragraphs in other datasets) and use distant supervision/multitask learning to address them. Related work coverage is described as thorough and prior methods’ performance is acknowledged where relevant.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: the TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing is compared against the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, the 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader is evaluated on SQuAD (reporting state-of-the-art results), and distant supervision plus multitask training is used to extend training to datasets without paragraphs. Mathematical derivations are not central and none are needed; hyperparameters and relevant baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA, competing readers) are reported, indicating a generally sound experimental methodology. Minor concerns remain about potential label noise from the distant-supervision paragraph alignment, and the paper relies on SQuAD performance despite acknowledging that SQuAD question distribution may not fully reflect open‑domain QA — statistical significance testing and more analysis of DS noise/generalization are not mentioned in the summary. Overall the technical claims are well supported with only modest methodological gaps that do not invalidate the main conclusions. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: a TF‑IDF retriever with bigram hashing reportedly outperforms the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, a 3‑layer bidirectional LSTM Document Reader (with aligned question embeddings and manual features) attains state‑of‑the‑art SQuAD performance, and distant supervision plus multitask training enables end‑to‑end QA over Wikipedia; evaluations use multiple datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) and report EM/F1 with hyperparameters. There are no mathematical proofs to assess. The experimental methodology is broadly sound—appropriate baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA, BiDAF, etc.) and realistic retrieval+reading setup—but the summary indicates potential weaknesses: no mention of statistical significance testing and possible label noise/assumptions introduced by distant supervision and limiting retrieval to top‑5 articles. These are minor issues that do not undermine the main conclusions, so the work is technically solid overall.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: the TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing is compared against the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, the 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader is evaluated on SQuAD (reporting state-of-the-art results), and distant supervision plus multitask training is used to extend training to datasets without paragraphs. Mathematical derivations are not central and none are needed; hyperparameters and relevant baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA, competing readers) are reported, indicating a generally sound experimental methodology. Minor concerns remain about potential label noise from the distant-supervision paragraph alignment, and the paper relies on SQuAD performance despite acknowledging that SQuAD question distribution may not fully reflect open‑domain QA — statistical significance testing and more analysis of DS noise/generalization are not mentioned in the summary. Overall the technical claims are well supported with only modest methodological gaps that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: a TF‑IDF retriever with bigram hashing reportedly outperforms the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API, a 3‑layer bidirectional LSTM Document Reader (with aligned question embeddings and manual features) attains state‑of‑the‑art SQuAD performance, and distant supervision plus multitask training enables end‑to‑end QA over Wikipedia; evaluations use multiple datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) and report EM/F1 with hyperparameters. There are no mathematical proofs to assess. The experimental methodology is broadly sound—appropriate baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA, BiDAF, etc.) and realistic retrieval+reading setup—but the summary indicates potential weaknesses: no mention of statistical significance testing and possible label noise/assumptions introduced by distant supervision and limiting retrieval to top‑5 articles. These are minor issues that do not undermine the main conclusions, so the work is technically solid overall.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (DrWiki, the TF-IDF + bigram retriever, the 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader, and the distant-supervision/multitask training) are stated early and clearly. Notation and method descriptions are consistent and well-defined — architecture details and hyperparameters (e.g., 3-layer bidirectional LSTMs, h=128, bigram hashing with murmur3) are reported. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and informative, and the overall writing quality is good, though a few minor presentation refinements would make the paper exceptional. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from introducing DrWiki, through the retrieval and Document Reader methodology, to experiments on SQuAD and open-domain datasets and a conclusion noting limitations. The main contributions are stated early (TF-IDF+bigram retriever, 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader, distant supervision/multitask training) and datasets, baselines, hyperparameters, and evaluation metrics are explicitly reported, which aids reproducibility. Notation and model details (e.g., 3-layer bidirectional LSTMs, h=128, bigram hashing via murmur3) are described, and writing quality is noted as good, though the summary does not mention figures or tables explicitly, suggesting only minor presentation gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (DrWiki, the TF-IDF + bigram retriever, the 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader, and the distant-supervision/multitask training) are stated early and clearly. Notation and method descriptions are consistent and well-defined — architecture details and hyperparameters (e.g., 3-layer bidirectional LSTMs, h=128, bigram hashing with murmur3) are reported. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and informative, and the overall writing quality is good, though a few minor presentation refinements would make the paper exceptional.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from introducing DrWiki, through the retrieval and Document Reader methodology, to experiments on SQuAD and open-domain datasets and a conclusion noting limitations. The main contributions are stated early (TF-IDF+bigram retriever, 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader, distant supervision/multitask training) and datasets, baselines, hyperparameters, and evaluation metrics are explicitly reported, which aids reproducibility. Notation and model details (e.g., 3-layer bidirectional LSTMs, h=128, bigram hashing via murmur3) are described, and writing quality is noted as good, though the summary does not mention figures or tables explicitly, suggesting only minor presentation gaps.

main_claims: Proposes DrWiki, a system that answers open-domain factoid questions using Wikipedia as the sole knowledge source by combining document retrieval and machine comprehension.
A simple inverted-index TF-IDF retriever enhanced with bigram hashing outperforms the built-in ElasticSearch-based Wikipedia Search API for retrieving relevant articles.

A multi-layer recurrent neural network Document Reader (3-layer bidirectional LSTMs with aligned question embeddings and manual features) achieves state-of-the-art results on the SQuAD machine comprehension benchmark.

Using distant supervision to associate paragraphs to QA pairs from datasets without paragraphs, and multitask learning on these datasets, yields an effective end-to-end open-domain QA system over Wikipedia.
_summary.methodology: Retrieve top-5 Wikipedia articles per question using a TF-IDF bag-of-words model augmented with bigram hashing (224 bins via murmur3), then run a Document Reader (3-layer bidirectional LSTMs, h=128, with aligned question embeddings and manual features) to predict answer spans; generate additional training data via distant supervision for datasets lacking paragraph-level annotations.
limitations: SQuAD dataset distribution is specific because questions were written given a paragraph, so it may not represent open-domain QA.
CuratedTREC, WebQuestions and WikiMovies contain only question-answer pairs without associated paragraphs and hence cannot be used to directly train the Document Reader.
Wikipedia is designed for humans, not machines, which poses challenges for machine reading and QA.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - open-domain question answering using Wikipedia
